featuredSetups.label: Identity balance
featuredSetups.description: Open a first-quadrant point with the squared-projection graph already in view.

setup.note: Use this when the unit-circle point and the squared-projection graph should stay tied together.

featuredSetups.label: Quadrant II balance
featuredSetups.description: Move the point left of the y-axis and watch the squared sum stay fixed anyway.

setup.note: This keeps the sign flip visible without hiding the identity.

pageIntro.definition: Keep one rotating point and its projections visible so the core trig identities stay tied to geometry instead of detached symbol rules.
pageIntro.keyTakeaway: Because the unit-circle radius is 1, every live point satisfies x^2 + y^2 = 1.
pageIntro.whyItMatters: The same picture explains the Pythagorean identity, complementary-angle swaps, and quadrant sign changes, so identities become geometry you can check instead of formulas to memorize.

paragraphs: Trig identities land more honestly when they stay on the same unit-circle bench as the rotating point. This page keeps the point, its projections, and a squared-projection graph visible together so the identities come from geometry instead of looking like detached symbol tricks.

On the unit circle the radius is always 1, so every live point satisfies x^2 + y^2 = 1. Once the same point is read as (cos theta, sin theta), the Pythagorean identity is forced by the picture. Complementary-angle swaps are geometric too: in the first quadrant, swapping the horizontal and vertical shadows swaps cosine and sine.

keyIdeas: Because the unit-circle radius is 1, every live point satisfies x^2 + y^2 = 1.
Replacing x with cos theta and y with sin theta gives cos^2 theta + sin^2 theta = 1.
Raw sine and cosine signs can change across quadrants, but the squared sum stays fixed because the distance from the origin does not change.
Complementary angles swap the horizontal and vertical projections in the first quadrant, which is why sine and cosine trade places.

commonMisconception.myth: Trig identities are mostly symbolic algebra, so the unit-circle picture is optional once the formulas are memorized.
correction: The identities come from the same geometry as the live projections.
If the point stays on the unit circle, the squared projections must still add to 1 even when the raw signs change.

workedExamples.title: Worked example
workedExamples.intro: Use the identity-balance preset so the point and the squared graph stay aligned.

items.title: Read one unit-circle identity case
items.prompt: For the identity-balance preset, what does the unit-circle point say about the core identity?

steps.label: 1. Read the live projections
steps.template: At about 53.1 deg, the unit-circle point is close to (0.60, 0.80), so cos theta is about 0.60 and sin theta is about 0.80.

steps.label: 2. Square the same two projections
steps.template: That gives cos^2 theta \approx 0.36 and sin^2 theta \approx 0.64.

steps.label: 3. Add the squared pieces
steps.template: The squared-projection graph shows the same result numerically: 0.36 + 0.64 = 1.00.

items.interpretationTemplate: The identity is not a separate algebra trick. It is the distance formula for one point that never leaves the unit circle.

applyAction.label: Apply the identity balance view
applyAction.observationHint: Keep the squared graph and the projection guides visible together so the geometry stays ahead of the notation.

miniChallenge.prompt: Move the point from Quadrant I into Quadrant II without changing its distance from the origin. What has to stay true about the squared projections?
miniChallenge.prediction: Decide whether the sign change should affect the squared sum or only the raw cosine sign.
miniChallenge.answer: The squared sum still has to stay equal to 1.
miniChallenge.explanation: Crossing into Quadrant II flips the raw cosine sign, but the point is still on the same unit circle, so the distance formula does not change.

equations.label: Distance rule on the unit circle
equations.meaning: Any point on the unit circle stays exactly one unit from the origin.

equations.label: Pythagorean identity
equations.meaning: Substituting x = cos theta and y = sin theta into the unit-circle distance rule gives the core identity.

equations.label: Complementary-angle swap
equations.meaning: In the first quadrant, complementary angles swap the horizontal and vertical projections of the same right-triangle geometry.

variableLinks.label: Angular speed
variableLinks.description: Changes how quickly the point sweeps through the circle without changing the identity itself.

variableLinks.label: Starting angle
variableLinks.description: Moves the same unit-circle point through new quadrants so the raw projections change while the identity stays fixed.

controls.label: Angular speed
controls.description: Controls how quickly the point rotates around the unit circle.

controls.label: Starting angle
controls.description: Sets where the point starts before the live rotation begins.

presets.label: Identity balance
presets.description: A first-quadrant point near the classic 3-4-5 triangle ratios.

presets.label: Complementary pair
presets.description: A shallower first-quadrant angle that swaps which projection is larger.

presets.label: Quadrant II balance
presets.description: A second-quadrant point shows the raw sign flip while the squared sum stays fixed.

overlays.label: Projection guides
overlays.shortDescription: Project the current point back to the x and y axes.
whatToNotice: The same horizontal and vertical shadows become the quantities being squared on the identity graph.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the identity attached to the visible geometry.

overlays.label: Angle marker
overlays.shortDescription: Show the current swept angle from the positive x-axis.
whatToNotice: Changing theta moves the same point without changing the unit-circle radius.

overlays.label: Quadrant sign map
overlays.shortDescription: Keep the quadrant sign pattern visible while the point moves.
whatToNotice: Raw cosine and sine signs can flip even while the squared sum stays flat.
overlays.whyItMatters: It separates sign changes from the identity itself.

overlays.label: Rotation trail
overlays.shortDescription: Show the swept arc from the positive x-axis to the current point.
whatToNotice: The point is always moving on the same radius-1 circle even while the projections trade size.
overlays.whyItMatters: It keeps the fixed distance from the origin visible.

graphs.label: Squared projections and their sum
graphs.description: Square the current cosine and sine projections over the same rotation so the identity stays visible while the raw signs change.

graphs.label: Cosine and sine traces
graphs.description: Keep the raw projections nearby so the squared graph never turns into detached algebra.

noticePrompts.title: What to notice
noticePrompts.intro: Keep the circle and the identity graph visible together.

items.text: The cosine-square and sine-square traces trade size, but their sum stays pinned at 1 because the point never leaves the unit circle.
Crossing into Quadrant II flips the raw cosine sign first, but squaring removes that sign while keeping the geometry honest.

predictionMode.title: Predict
predictionMode.intro: Predict the identity outcome before you move the point into a new quadrant.

items.prompt: If the point moves into Quadrant II while staying on the unit circle, what should happen to cos^2 theta + sin^2 theta?
items.scenarioLabel: Move the point into Quadrant II

choices.label: It should become less than 1 because cosine turns negative.
It should become greater than 1 because sine stays positive.

items.explanation: The point is still one unit from the origin, so the squared projections still add to 1 even though the raw cosine sign changes.
items.observationHint: Watch the raw projection traces and the squared-sum line together.

challengeMode.title: Challenge mode
challengeMode.intro: Use the unit-circle point, the projection guides, and the squared graph together. This checkpoint is about building a concrete identity case from one live angle instead of quoting the formula cold.

items.title: Three-four-five identity checkpoint
items.prompt: Start in Quadrant I and set the point so cosine is near 0.6 while sine is near 0.8. Keep the squared-projection graph open so the identity line stays in view while you tune the angle.
items.successMessage: You built a clean 3-4-5-style unit-circle case. The raw projections now sit near 0.6 and 0.8, and the squared graph still locks their sum at 1.

setup.note: Aim for a first-quadrant point a little steeper than 45 degrees.

hints: The point should stay in Quadrant I, so both raw projections must remain positive.
You want an angle closer to 60 degrees than to 30 degrees.

targets.label: Set the starting angle between about 0.89 and 0.96 rad so the projections land near 0.6 and 0.8.

quickTest.title: Quick test
quickTest.intro: Answer from the live point and the squared graph.

questions.prompt: What is the cleanest geometric source of cos^2 theta + sin^2 theta = 1 on this bench?

choices.label: It is the distance rule x^2 + y^2 = 1 for one point on the unit circle.
It only works at special angles like 30, 45, and 60 degrees.
It comes from averaging the sine and cosine graphs.

questions.explanation: The identity is just the unit-circle distance formula rewritten in trig language.

showMeAction.label: Show the identity balance
showMeAction.observationHint: The fixed sum line comes from the point staying one unit from the origin.

selectedWrongExplanations.b: The identity holds for every point on the unit circle, not only special angles.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: The squared-sum identity comes from circle geometry, not graph averaging.

questions.prompt: If the point crosses from Quadrant I into Quadrant II, what changes first?

choices.label: The squared-sum line drops below 1 because cosine becomes negative.
The raw cosine sign flips while the squared-sum line stays at 1.
The raw sine sign flips first while cosine stays positive.

questions.explanation: Crossing the y-axis flips the horizontal projection sign first, but squaring keeps the identity balanced.

selectedWrongExplanations.a: The squared-sum stays 1 even when one coordinate becomes negative.
selectedWrongExplanations.c: Sine stays positive when moving from Quadrant I to Quadrant II.

paragraphs: The simulation shows a unit circle with one rotating point, horizontal and vertical projection guides, an angle marker, a quadrant sign map, and graphs that compare the raw projections with their squared values.
One graph shows cosine and sine changing over time, and a second graph shows cosine squared, sine squared, and their sum staying fixed at 1.

seo.title: Trig Identities from Unit-Circle Geometry
seo.description: Use one rotating unit-circle point and its squared projections to see why the core trig identities come from geometry instead of detached symbol rules.
keywords: cos squared plus sin squared equals 1

v2.intuition: Trig identities are distance facts about one point: cosine is the horizontal projection, sine is the vertical projection, and the squared projections must add back to the fixed radius.
v2.whyItMatters: When the circle, projections, signs, and squared graph stay linked, you can explain why an identity survives angle changes instead of relying on symbol rules alone.

equationSnapshot.note: Read x^2 + y^2 = 1 from the radius first, substitute x = cos θ and y = sin θ second, then use first-quadrant projection swaps for complementary-angle identities.

guidedSteps.title: Read squared projections
guidedSteps.goal: Tie the Pythagorean identity to one visible unit-circle point before moving the angle.
guidedSteps.doThis: Apply Identity balance, keep Projection guides and Rotation trail visible, and compare the point near (0.60, 0.80) with the squared-projection graph.
guidedSteps.notice: Cosine and sine are the two shadows of the same radius-1 point. Squaring the shadows gives about 0.36 and 0.64, and the sum line stays at 1.
guidedSteps.explain: The identity starts as the distance rule x^2 + y^2 = 1. Reading the same point as (cos θ, sin θ) turns that distance rule into cos^2 θ + sin^2 θ = 1.

guidedSteps.title: Separate sign from square
guidedSteps.goal: See why a raw sign flip does not break the squared identity.
guidedSteps.doThis: Switch to Quadrant II balance, keep the Quadrant sign map on, and watch raw cosine become negative while the squared-sum graph remains pinned at 1.
guidedSteps.notice: Crossing the y-axis changes the sign of the horizontal projection first, but the distance from the origin and the squared sum do not change.
guidedSteps.explain: Squaring removes the raw sign while preserving projection length. The identity depends on the fixed radius, not on which quadrant supplies the signs.

guidedSteps.title: Swap complementary angles
guidedSteps.goal: Use first-quadrant geometry to explain why sine and cosine trade places for complementary angles.
guidedSteps.doThis: Use Complementary pair, then move Starting angle within Quadrant I and compare which projection is longer before and after the angle is made shallower or steeper.
guidedSteps.notice: In a right-triangle view of the same radius, the horizontal leg for one acute angle becomes the vertical leg for its complement.
guidedSteps.explain: Complementary-angle identities are not new facts to memorize. They come from the same two projection lengths being read from opposite acute angles in the first quadrant.

guidedSteps.title: Build an identity case
guidedSteps.goal: Synthesize the point, graph, signs, and formula in one concrete angle check.
guidedSteps.doThis: Use the quick checks, then open Challenge mode and tune the first-quadrant point until cosine is near 0.6 and sine is near 0.8.
guidedSteps.notice: A complete explanation names the point, its two projections, the squared pieces, and the fixed radius-1 sum.
guidedSteps.explain: The formula becomes reliable when every part of the page agrees: the circle gives the point, the guides give cosine and sine, and the squared graph checks the distance rule.

learned: Every point on the unit circle satisfies x^2 + y^2 = 1 because the radius is fixed at 1.
Reading that same point as (cos θ, sin θ) gives cos^2 θ + sin^2 θ = 1.
Raw sine or cosine signs can flip across quadrants, but the squared identity survives because the distance from the origin is unchanged.
Complementary first-quadrant angles swap horizontal and vertical projections, so sine and cosine trade roles.
wrapUp.misconception: Do not treat trig identities as detached algebra rules. Keep the point, projections, and radius visible so each identity has a geometric reason.

nextConcepts.title: Inverse Trig / Angle from Ratio
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Recover angles from ratios while keeping the quadrant signs that made the identity honest.

nextConcepts.title: Parametric Curves / Motion from Equations
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Carry sine and cosine projections into motion where x(t) and y(t) trace a curve.

nextConcepts.title: Derivative as Slope / Local Rate of Change
nextConcepts.reasonLabel: Reuse trig relationships when changing quantities become local rates.

wrapUp.freePlayPrompt: Pick any quadrant, predict which raw projection signs should change, then move Starting angle and use the squared graph to check that the identity still adds to 1.
